title: Aliasing
text: This lesson shows a continous “analog” signal being converted to a digital signal by an ADC (Analog-to-Digital Converter). What we can observe from the results is the fact that a poorly sampled signal can become indistinguishable from a lower-frequency one.

title: Aliasing
text: This lesson shows a continous “analog” signal being converted to a digital signal by an ADC (Analog-to-Digital Converter). What we can observe from the results is the fact that a poorly sampled signal can become indistinguishable from a lower-frequency one.

title: Aliasing
text:
Setup and Parameters
First, we import our tools and define the physical properties of our signal and ADC.


Code
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

f_real = 6000.0   # It's too fast (Greater than f_sample/2)
f_sample = 8000.0 # Our ADC is still at 8000 Hz
f_alias = 2000.0  # The "Ghost" signal the computer will see (|6000 - 8000|)

title: Aliasing
text:
Signal Simulation
We simulate the “continuous” analog world using a high-resolution array, and the digital world by sampling at discrete intervals.


Code
# 1. Simulate the "continuous" analog world
t_analog = np.linspace(0, 0.002, 1000) 
y_analog_real = np.sin(2 * np.pi * f_real * t_analog)

# 2. Simulate the digital world (discrete sampled points)
t_digital = np.arange(0, 0.002, 1.0 / f_sample)
y_digital = np.sin(2 * np.pi * f_real * t_digital)

# 3. Simulate the "alias" wave
y_analog_alias = -np.sin(2 * np.pi * f_alias * t_analog)

title: Aliasing
text:
Visualization
Finally, we plot the results to see how the samples align with the signals.
Visually the concept becomes very clear: the system will have no way of knowing if the blue samples reconstruct the gray signal or the red signal.


Code
plt.figure(figsize=(10, 5))

# Plot the real fast wave
plt.plot(t_analog, y_analog_real, color='gray', alpha=0.5, label=f"Real Analog Signal ({f_real} Hz)")

# Plot the alias slow wave
plt.plot(t_analog, y_analog_alias, color='red', linestyle='--', label=f"Aliased Signal ({f_alias} Hz)")

# Plot the discrete samples
plt.stem(t_digital, y_digital, basefmt=" ", linefmt="blue", markerfmt="bo", label=f"Digital Samples ({f_sample} Hz)")

plt.title("Time-Domain Proof of Aliasing")
plt.xlabel("Time (s)")
plt.ylabel("Amplitude")
plt.legend()
plt.grid(True)
plt.show()








Figure 1: Time-Domain Proof of Aliasing

title: Aliasing
text:
The Nyquist Theorem
What we have demonstrated is the Nyquist-Shannon Sampling Theorem. It states that to capture a signal without losing information, we must sample at a rate (\(f_s\)) that is at least twice the highest frequency component of the signal (\(f_{max}\)).
\[f_s > 2 \cdot f_{max}\]
When we sampled a \(6000\text{ Hz}\) signal at only \(8000\text{ Hz}\), we violated this rule because \(8000\) is not greater than \(2 \times 6000\).

title: Aliasing
text:
Extras: Moiré Pattern
Aliasing is not just about signals: it’s about cameras, audio, and video games. It can be represented visually using the Moiré pattern.
For me, looking at this animation made the concept really click:






Figure 2: Moiré Pattern

title: OOP in IoT
text: As we have established, OOP is everywhere. Let’s delve into what problems it solves in the context of IoT and why it’s so important for modern connected systems.

title: OOP in IoT
OOP in IoT
text:
OOP is Intuitive
OOP is naturally suited for Human–Computer Interaction (HCI).
Because OOP models real-world objects, it provides an inherently intuitive framework for developers to map physical IoT devices (like lights, thermostats, and locks) to the user’s mental model, making it easier to create truly user-centric applications.
Human-Computer Interaction (HCI) is governed by UI/UX and OOP is applied to the backend and domain logic in this context, making the backend object-oriented promotes a more seamless interaction with an user-centric front-end.
However, this interaction is only possible if we define a bridging mechanism; we will discuss how API Symmetry and Domain-Driven Design accomplish this in later articles.





flowchart LR
    User([User]) -->|Interacts with| Frontend[User-Centric Front-end]
    Frontend <-->|API Symmetry / DDD| Backend[Backend Domain Logic]
    Backend <-->|Controls| Devices[/Physical IoT Devices/]

title: OOP in IoT
text:
OOP and memory
Memory is precious, especially in IoT where devices have very limited RAM.
Unfortunately, dynamic polymorphism in languages like C++ relies on virtual method tables (v-tables), which introduce pointer indirection and memory overhead.
In heavily constrained embedded systems, strict OOP is often abandoned in favor of procedural C or statically dispatched paradigms (like Rust traits) to save cycles and bytes.

title: OOP in IoT
text:
OOP + IoT + ML
A primary focus for the future of IoT is its integration with AI and ML models (TinyML). How OOP interacts with this depends entirely on the hardware tier.
On higher-level implementations (like IoT Gateways) running full operating systems, OOP and HCI-OOP frameworks can treat complex predictive ML algorithms as “objects” or plugins. This allows the system to dynamically load and seamlessly upgrade its intelligence at runtime without rewriting core infrastructure.
However, on microcontrollers, polymorphically injecting or hot-swapping ML models at runtime is impossible. Memory is strictly bound.
On hardware like the STM32 Nucleo (which I use extensively), standard OOP-heavy runtimes are often discarded. Even generic interpreters like TensorFlow Lite for Microcontrollers (TFLM) incur too much overhead. Instead, the industry standard relies on static compilation using silicon-specific tools like STM32Cube.AI (X-CUBE-AI).
X-CUBE-AI takes a trained neural network and compiles it directly into highly optimized, bare-metal C code. It strips away the interpreter, maps operations directly to hardware accelerators, and statically links the model into the firmware. At the edge, ML is not a dynamically dispatched object; it is a statically evaluated, procedural function call.





flowchart TD
    subgraph Gateway [IoT Gateway Tier - Dynamic]
        A[Core System] -->|Polymorphic Call| B(ML Plugin Interface)
        B -.-> C[Anomaly Detector Object]
        B -.-> D[Predictive Maintenance Object]
    end

    subgraph EdgeNode [STM32 Edge Tier - Static]
        E[STM32Cube.AI] -->|Compiles to| F[Bare-metal C Code]
        G[Main Loop] -->|Static Function Call| F
    end







Conclusion
In conclusion, I believe OOP is not merely a paradigm that can be applied to IoT, but rather an indivisible part of it.
It is not a question of if we should apply the paradigm, or even why. Because OOP is such an inherent part of modeling IoT systems, our only focus is to study exactly how its core concepts apply to the physical devices we build.
Ultimately, the industry is called the Internet of Things for a reason. When we recognize that these physical ‘Things’ are inherently software ‘Objects’, IoT becomes, quite literally, an Internet of Objects.

title: Learning Object-Oriented Programming
text:
Object Oriented Programming is one of the most important paradigms in software engineering.
It’s imporant to note that this isn’t intended to be an highly academic resource or even a scientific research; these are just my notes and experiences, occasionally conveyed in an informal manner.

title: Welcome to the Signal Theory Lab
text:
Welcome to the Signal Theory Lab
This is a digital playground for exploring signal processing concepts.

Core Topics

Aliasing
Frequency Analysis (Coming soon)
Filter Design (Coming soon)



Environment
The environment is built using: - NumPy for numerical computation. - SciPy for signal processing algorithms. - Matplotlib for visualization. - Quarto for document generation.
